Hey Boss,
Issue No. 14 • September 2012
How To Pick Up Chicks
(And Lots More New Custom.ers)
WithEm.ail
Back in 2009 one of my clients at the time (who does $20 million+ in sales last I
heard) and I got to talking about our favorite sales and persuasion books.
One of the books he recommended was:
"The G8.1lle"
It's by Neil Strauss. And, it's about the underground pick up artist society. Very
fascinating book. And it's packed with lots of real-life sales and marketing applications
I've used over and over and over in my sales letters, emails, articles, blog posts and even
during interviews when pitching my products.
Anyway, that book led to others similar books, sites and products.
And those resources led to others... then those resources led to others...

-- 1 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
And you get the picture.
(It's quite a "rabbit hole", actually.)
Anyway, one of those resources is a fascinating blog by a PUA who calls himself
"Roosh V". The dude can write a mean blog post, to say the least. And recently, he was
giving advice on his "program" for eager young whipper snappers who want to get into
the pick up artist game. (BTw, don't get hung up on the morality of this stuff, at the end
of the day it's simply a tool-- a tool that can be used for GOOD or EVIL, so use wisdom
with this stuff, yo?)
Where was I?
Ah yes, Roosh's blog posts. A few months ago, he wrote a "fast start" checklist for
newbies. You can read it here:
www.rooshv.com/the-roosh-program
And guess what?
This EXACT routine (almost down to the letter) applies to MASTERING email
marketing, too. In fact, while reading it, I remember doing practically ALL of these
things when starting out and it's paid off BIG time. Below is that checklist Gust the bullets,
there is more to it than this) with my commentary:
1. Start a workout routine.
Roosh was talking about working out physically (weights, etc). But for email, the
"workout" is writing each day. You cannot get around this, babycakes. Writers write. Well,
at least writers who want to get paid ...
2. Get your hands on a game resource.
When learning email I sought out all the products I could. Ultimately I learned
most of my email "game" fundamentals from the One Resource that rules them all (Matt
Furey's ORIGINAL email course, do not ask me how to get it, I don't know) and then
going forth and DOING what I was learning about. Eventually, after mastering the
-2-
www.BenSettle.com 	• 	Copyright 20 12 Ben Settle, All Rights Reserved 	• 	Published By Settle, LLC
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 28 --

T H _E 	EMAIL 	P LAY E R S 	N E W S LETTER
fundamentals, I started developing my own style and system that varies greatly from MF's.
And that's how you want it to be. Slavishly learn the fundamentals, then start adapting
and tweaking as you see fit -- for your personality, products, market, and situation. In the
end, you'll be much better off. But in the beginning master the fundamentals. Only then,
an Email Player will you be.
3. Do 100 approaches.
Roosh is talking about approaching 100 women to get started. It's interesting how,
for email marketing, I often tell people virtually the same thing: "Vrite a daily email for 90
days (90 "approaches"). At the very least do the 30-day challenge outlined in "The Email
Players Cookbook". By doing this you quickly get comfortable with writing. You quickly
get comfortable with selling. And you quickly get comfortable with the idea of money
rolling in regularly (believe it or not, people have hang ups with this and end up
sabotaging themselves, which is another story for another day. .. )
4. Watch one episode of Seinfeld a day.
Yep, even in the original Street-Smart Email system (and now in your "Email
Players Cookbook") I recommend watching Seinfeld. I consider it THE most important
show for email marketers to watch if you want to learn the fine art of writing "an email
about nothing'' (but that also helps sell your product and doesn't waste your reader's
time).
5. Limit JDasturbation to only one day a week.
OK, I didn't do this part. Being married at the time, it was irrelevant. But, there
IS a lot of power in abstaining froJD your :fteshly desires when you want to
JDaster a new skill. It's not something you hear a lot about. But doing this (especially
abstaining from sex/masturbation-- hey, we're not afraid to speaketh the troof around
here ...) keeps your mind focused and your concentration razor sharp in ways you won't
believe. This can also include long stretches of time (7, 14, 21, 30 days ... or more ...) going
without sex, junk food, TV (except for Seinfeld, of course), farting around on social media
or whatever it is that you spend all your time doing that gets in the way of writing emails.
Not only will your brain reward you with more ideas, creativity and mental energy. .. but
you'll develop a kind of discipline none of your competition will likely have. It's kind of
hard to explain, you just have to do this to understand it. Be the "master of your
-3-

-- 3 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
domain" (hat tip to Seinfeld ... ), and you'll be a master of growing your business.
6. Write down notes and observations in a dedicated player notebook.
Once again, this is mandatory. When I started writing daily emails (and I still do
this when writing for the weight loss market) you should be noting which appeals are
working best and how consistently. Also, realize just because something didn't work one
time, doesn't mean it won't again (with a change of subject line, etc) and vice versa. This
is why paying attention is so important.
7. Read one new book every two weeks.
In my case, I went through Furey's course over and over and over. Frankly, during
that time period of 2008-2011 I would alternate between his and Gary Bencivenga's
copywriting course. At the moment, I've gone through both around 20 times (yes, I keep
track). That endless repetition ingrains all the concepts and ideas and
strategies into your brain. And all that information then starts feeding your
subconscious which then hands you (on a silver platter) ideas for your projects and ads
and emails you'd never have gotten otherwise.
8. After your 100 approaches, take two weeks ofT to reflect.
I think this is important for email, as well. Especially when starting out. Write your
90 or emails FAST (not 1 email per day, but 4-5 or MORE per day). Then put them in
your auto-responder or pre-load into your broadcasting software (all the major ones let
you do this) and relax for a bit. Note the results your emails get (which ones people buy
from or respond to with questions, comments, etc).
Let all the results "gel."
Then, get back to it. You'll be amazed at how much better you get.
9. Repeat steps 3 through 8 while still working out.
'nuff said.
Anyway, go ye forth and follow these steps. Yes, even if you're not a "newbie." I do

them myself even now. And it's a great discipline that will serve you well the rest of your
days ...
Your Uncle Ben
Wants YOU, 	Soldier...
I want to start a new tradition in these golden pages of wisdom. Instead of just
publishing my own stories, ideas, emails and strategies... I want to start publishing some of
YOURS, too. I was thinking, it'd be extremely educational if "Email Players" subscribers
started sending in your best, highest selling emails for the benefit of the email playerhood.
You can also do some righteous bragging, too, of course. (And I'd be disappointed if you
didn't!)
What kinds of submissions?
Well, if you wrote an email that did exceptionally well, send it in . .It may or may
not be published in this newsletter, depending on how many submissions I get. But if
nothing else I may be able to use it to illustrate a lesson in the future. And, of course,
you'll get full credit (including your URL, if you want).
Also, feel free to add your notes and any details that are relevant.
Then I'll publish the best ones in each issue (with your links intact, of course) along
with any commentary or thoughts I may have. This way we can all get our learn on by
being exposed to other peoples' successes. If you're interested, just shoot your best selling
email over to ben@bensettle.coDl along with any info you think we should know
before reading it. Also, if you have emails that did exceptionally BAD, those are game,
too. Mter all, sometimes we can learn more from what NOT to do than we can learn
from what TO do. Like I've said before, my goal for this newsletter is for subscribers to be
the BEST out there. To write emails that allow you to kick dust into the face of anyone
who dares compete against you. And every tip, tactic or idea that helps further that goal
are included in the hallowed pages of this newsletter.
So send 'em on in, and let's see what ya got, kid ...

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
How To Laugh Your Way
All The Way To The Bank
There's SO much power in using humor in emails. (And not just emails, I'm
finding it to be powerful in other selling as well, but especially in emails). Now, I wrote
about this in depth earlier this year in the January issue (back issues are available, just
PayPal $97 to "ben@bensettle.com" -- make sure you include your mailing address and
the issue you want (say 'january 2012 infotainment" or something like that) in the
message field PayPal gives you). The only thing to realize is often times if you do a
REALLY effective job, you may not make many or any sales that day. And that's okay,
because that's not the point. Having the long view in mind is key here -- when you make
people laugh (yes, LAUGH, as in out loud, coffee spit all over the keyboard) you will have
bonded yourself to that individual in a way where he/ she will now want to KEEP reading
your emails forever and ever and ever. Humor is almost like a drug. (And I would not be
surprised if chemicals mixed inside our brains to prove this.) The more we laugh, and feel
good about it, the more we want another "taste."
So with emails the sale is great if you make it.
But if not, don't fret.
Because it's about long term bonding and readership (the longer your reader sticks
around and the more emails they eagerly read, the more likely they are to buy, in many
cases). Here's an example below. Note that this email has NO lesson, NO value, and NO
real sales pitch or reason to buy. It's just a story people always find funny when I tell it.
And the feedback to it was incredible. I got a few sales, yes, but most importantly, I got
more long term readership.
Here is the email:
Subject: 	Fart your way to success
I ever tell you about the time I 	farted in study hall?
It was kind of a hair raising experience.
-6-

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
And, while the following probably won't make you any smarter, 	you
may find it useful if you see the lesson "between the lines."
Here's what happened:
I was in se7enth grade, 	and it was 8th period 	(the last period of
the day). 	I was sitting there, 	lost in a book I was reading when,
all the sudden -- RIIIIPl 	-- the fart popped off without warning --
with the noise ricocheting off my wooden seat and around the giant
"echo-friendly" room.
Laughter erupted and everyone turned my way.
My 13-year-old heart raced and my mind reeled.
How am I 	gonna get out of THIS mess?
Luckily, 	I 	always had a talent for wiggling my way out of trouble
(like detentions, 	doing chores, 	etc) 	and did the first thing that
came to mind:
I 	turned and looked at the kid BEHIND me as if he did it.
Did it work?
You bet it did!
And everyone laughed at HIM instead. 	(Wasn't I 	a little stinker?)
OK, 	so what's the point?
To blame others when you make a stink of things?
Actually, 	I'm not sure there even is a point.
Except for maybe when you make a loud noise, 	people notice.
Have a nice weekend.
Ben Settle
P.S. 	For dozens of ways to make a big, 	fat noise in your market,
and get lots and lots of buyers checking out your products and
services 	(while ignoring everyone else), 	toot on over to:
http://www.CopywritingGrabBag.com

-- 7 of 28 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
See? No real lesson (not really, at least). No real point. No real substance. just a
humorous (yes, real life) story that gave people a chuckle and smile. And that, my friend,
can bond you to your readers in ways none of the super goo-roo tricks ever will.
Wait a minute.
What?
You are saying you can't do this?
And you are saying you can't do this because you are not naturally funny or don't
have any funny stories to share?
Worry ye not! Because there is a very easy way to "cheat" with this: Use OTHER
peoples' stories. You don't have to be a comedian to repeat a joke, after all, do you? Of
course not. So here's what you do. Go to this site: www.fmylife.com.. Not only will it
make you feel pretty good about YOUR life (you'll see why) but you'll find some of the
best, funniest stories (some real, some fake, but it doesn't matter) you'll ever see. All there
for you, on a golden platter. I can spend HOURS on this site. It's just a fascinating read as
people tell stories about crappy things that happened to them. And make no mistake,
people like this. It's why screenwriters for movies work hard to evoke SYMPATHY in
their characters.
Well, guess what?
You can do thou likewise.
Just go to this above site and note the funny stories, start turning them into emails
using what you learn in this newsletter and have a blast. Also, don't get too hung up on
the lying aspect. You don't have to lie. Just say you "heard about this guy who ... " so no
problem there. It's all true and you have a clean conscience. This is mega powerful stuff
my friend. Humor like this not only gets attention and bonds people to you, but it lowers
your readers' "sales defenses", makes them comfortable with you. And when people arc
comfy, they are far more likely to give your products a try.

How To Use Amazon Kindle
And The ''Lowly'' E1nail
To Explode Your Sales, Leads
And Product Testi1nonials
I'm a big fan of using Kindle these days. Not just because of the sales, but because
those Kindle books can make great lead generators (although, I am finding ~nany of
the buyers are now "conditioned" to only spend a couple dollars on a book,
so sharp eJDail skillz are a JDust if you want to convert those leads into
buyers). Anyway, a little while back I used email to boost my book rankings and increase
long terms sales (and leads). You can do the same thing and, yes, I'm going to show you
right now! One thing Kindle lets you do every now and then is give your product away
free for a limited time. I don't pretend to know exactly how this works, as my Kindle
publisher took care of it for me. But I am sure it's easy enough to find on the Kindle
website. Or, just find a Kindle publisher (there are many if you Google them) and see if
they can help you with this and publish your books.
Anyway, here's what happened:
One day I was looking at my Kindle books and noticed some yahoo left a bad
review on my "Copywriters Crib Sheet" book on Kindle. Just a pointlessly negative and
ignorant review from some dark who probably lives in his mom's basement eating
Cheetos and watching porn all day. So I got this idea:
"Ben, why don't you ask your CopywritingGrabBag.com list who all got that exact
book free when they opted in to leave reviews?" Here is the email (pretty simple, and you
can model (hellz, SWIPE) it exactly if you sell whatever your freebie is on Kindle
anyway):
Subject: question for you
%$firstname$%,
At one time you opted in to my list at www.copywritinggrabbag.com.
When you did that, 	you should have received the free "Copywriters

Crib Sheet" eBook.
If not, 	you can download it here:
http://www.CopywritingGrabBag.com/eBook-download
Anyway, 	I wanted to ask you a favor.
I am selling this book on Amazon's Kindle and someone left a
dorky review on there.
Now, 	usually, 	I 	don't care about such things.
This guy sounds like he didn't even read it anyway.
But, 	it got me to thinking.
I've gotten all kinds of testimonials about this eBook, 	but I 	can't
post them in amazon's reviews, 	only other users can.
So here's my question:
If you read the book ••. 	and if it helped you •••
Would you be willing to leave a review?
If not, 	it's no big deal.
Really, 	I 	understand.
But if it helped you in any way, 	shape or form, 	and if you
are willing to leave a review, 	here is where to do it:
http://amzn.to/ylq49I
Thank you in advance.
I 	really appreciate it •••
Ben Settle
Pretty simple stuff.
And boy did the reviews pour in! Only problem was, as my boy (and Email Players
subscriber) Sanjay Pan de said, "Ben, why don't you give the book away free on
-10-

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Kindle instead, otherwise Amazon will.find all these reviews by non-buyers
fishy." Duh, Ben! So I quickly emailed my Kindle guy and he said not to fret, he put it
up as free for 5 days. Then, the next few days I emailed that list (about 600 people or so)
saying they could download it free on amazon and thus, verified buyers were leaving
reviews (all this is important to realize when selling on Kindle, they have to watch the
integrity of their system and make sure fake reviews are kept to a minimum for obvious
reasons).
Anyway, long story short?
Lots of reviews!
And, to spice it up even more (and give more incentive to download it
free) I said the most interesting review would get a free issue of "Email
Players." The result was some of the best (and honest, no bull crap) reviews I could
have hoped for. The cheeto idiot's review was relegated to the last page (barely even
registering on anyone's radar) and my rankings went way up, making it more visible and
increasing long term sales (and leads).
All because of the lowly email.
Note: I am not sure what I did was in line with Kindle's rules as far as "most
interesting review" or whatever. So do that at your own risk. But nevertheless, you can, of
course, follow my exact model above (and you should). In fact, I am going to be doing this
sort of thing more with my main list, not just my sublist of copywriters. It's a great way to
kill multiple birds with one pebble: Product testimonials, increased sales, lead generation
(if you put links to your site in your books, of course) and a tighter bond with your
customers.
Lots of positives, no negatives, booyah!

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
I mentioned this in "The Email Players Cookbook" -- that radio talk show host
Michael Savage is a GREAT example of how email can and should be done. Even if
you hate his politics, he's easily one of the best natural conununicators of
our age. His storytelling ability is crazy powerful, and perfect for emails. But, one other
thing he does extremely well is ... sell his books on the air. You see, Savage isn't backed by
the usual big media outlets like Fox News, etc. Most of the other talk radio hosts ignore
him, too. In many ways, it's almost like he doesn't exist... except to his own audience
(which is ginormous -- he's the #3 biggest radio talk show host last I heard). Anyway, so
he doesn't really get a lot of PR or anything. Yet, his books fly off shelves. And a big part
of that is the way he uses his radio show to blatantly (but tastefully) pitch his books. He
does it like a champ, and I am always studying how he does that on his show in my
emails. One of those ways is sim.ply reading excerpts. Pretty, simple, eh? Only
thing is, he doesn't just read your excerpts randomly. You can tell he very carefully picks
out 'WHICH excerpts to read so that, yes, he gives you lots of "meat" you can chew on,
but he leaves you on a such a cliff-hanger it makes it very difficult for his fanboys to not
rush out to buy the book. Personally, I don't do this NEARLY enough (NOTE TO SELF:
WTF IS WRONG, MAN? GET TO IT!) but when I do, the sales come in much faster
that particular day. For example, here is how I once used an excerpt of a bonus report to
sell "Email Players":
Subject: 	Internet shocker -- how split testing HURTS sales
Hey, 	so this should rile up the goo-roo fanboys •••
Check this out:
I'm going to do an unusual test on my capture pages this week I
anticipate will drive up my opt-ins quite a bit over time.
What makes it unusual?
Because this test involves leaving the LOSING pages upl
Yes, 	mi amigo, 	you read that right.
I 	learned it from my computer scientist pal.
(The one I've been telling you about lately.)
He LAUGHS at traditional split testing.

In fact, 	here's how he put it:
"A lot of gurus who talk about their split tests, 	it is parroting
in the first place because someone else told them they ought to be
split testing. 	They don't really split test. 	They run one test once
and then they go, 	•we tested ••• and this big yellow frickin' 	button
is the best-performing button out of all the buttons we tested.'
The reality is they don't even understand the basic thing about the
way they're measuring the split test. 	They don't understand the
statistics behind it. They don't have any proper grasp of the
scientific rigor that would go into an actual real scientific
experiment, 	the procedure you go through to make sure that when you
come to a conclusion, 	this really is correct, 	not just some radical
guesswork that you justify."
Amusing, 	isn't it?
But don't let his science talk scare you.
The method he DOES use is actually easier in many ways than the
split testing everyone else does.
Perfect for newbies and luddites alike.
And it's great for both squeeze and sales pages.
Want to learn it?
Then get ye bootyus on the "Email Players" 	list.

transcript with that computer scientist, 	and he goes into detail
about how to do a proper online test.

See how that works? I did not give away the actual secret. But, I gave away enough
meat to keep people intrigued and wanting to buy (and I remember that email making
way more sales than I usually would get during that time of the month selling my
newsletter). You can do the same thing. Find snippets of your product and quote them in
your emails. Make sure they are inherently valuable snippets of info, of course. But that
they arc incomplete, and more of a tease of greater things to come. Do this and you will
have peace the rest of your days ...
How To Use Long Words
To Make Big Sales
In Short E~nails
One of the all-time great copywriting books (in my humble, but accurate, opinion)
is Eugene Schwartz's classic "Breakthrough Advertising". There are hundreds of tips and
insights inside that book. And many of them are so un-sexy on the surface you will hardly
ever see them discussed by the goo-roohood.
One of those lessons is what Gene called "the language of logic."
This is where you pepper (strategically, don't overdo it) words and language that
have "built-in" credibility. They sound very logical and intelligent. And are often used in
courts, scientific circles, legal arguments, etc. And if you pepper your emails with these
words every so often (again, don't overdo it, especially in emails) you can let thousands of
years of linguistic credibility rub off on your bad self.
On the next page is a list I put together a few years ago. It's not exhaustive (there's
a word for ya) by any means. But it serves as a starting point. To add to it, pay attention to
court, academic and scientific language and see if you can't work their words and phrases
into your emails:
-14-

Now before 	I 	explain what the King of Selling Online is, 	I 	want to

king," 	"This is 	king," 	"This is the best thing, 	this 	is the most

First I 	want to say that one of the big things 	that people think is
King of Selling Online is teaching, 	and it's not. 	I'm not saying
teaching is not important. 	I'm not saying you shouldn't do teaching
online, 	but it's not the King of Selling Online, 	and I 	can prove it.

get paid very, 	very little money, 	if any. 	They've never gotten paid a

teachers don't get paid that much. 	Again I'm not saying it's not
important. 	It is 	important, 	but 	it's not king.
Another thing that people think is the King of Selling Online is
giving value. 	Again, 	is 	it important? 	Yes. 	Should you do it?
Absolutely. 	Everything you do should be valuable. 	Whenever you contact
anyone you're trying to sell to, 	it should be full of that, 	but it's
not the King of Selling online, 	and I 	can prove that too.
I 	know a 	lot of people out there, 	and you probably know people out
there - 	you may be doing this yourself; 	creating a 	lot of value and

You even see people creating what they call "free lines," where

Another thing you'll hear people say, 	mostly copywriters -which I 	am
one~ 	I'm first 	and foremost a direct response copywriter - 	and a 	lot
of copywriters will say, 	"Copywriting is 	king, 	because nothing gets
done until you have the copywriting done. 	Without a 	good ad it won't

I'm going to have to respectfully disagree with my colleagues on that.

someone who's written a 	lot of ads 	for 	a 	lot of different markets, 	for

on my website or on my email newsletter knows 	this. 	I 	talk about it

Actually, 	the biggest lesson I 	learned about selling came from the UPS
man. 	You see, 	unlike most people, 	I 	didn't buy my comics at the comic
book store. 	I 	actually did it through mail order. 	I 	was a big fan of
mail order even back before I 	was 	selling with mail order.
What I 	used to do is, 	I 	used to order my comics from this one place,
and I 	don't know if they're still around. 	I 	don't even remember their
name, 	quite frankly, 	but 	just to give you an idea of how powerful the
principle I'm going to teach you today is, 	once a month, 	usually
around the 12th or 15th, 	the UPS man would come and drop off this big
box of comic books that 	I'd ordered.
Now I 	could have bought these comics 	for 	the same price, 	and maybe
even cheaper, 	down at the local comic book store, 	and I 	would have got
them a couple weeks earlier at 	least in most cases. 	A lot of people
would think, 	"Why would you do this? Why would you go out of your way
to order comics through mail when you could have got them potentially
cheaper at the comic book store, 	and two or three weeks 	faster?"

Well, 	in this big box of comics would be a catalog, 	this 	little
cheaply made, 	stapled together, 	8x5 	little catalog of all the comics
they were selling for the next month, 	sort of 	like an editorial review
of all the comics that were coming up, 	usually 	just information that
the companies 	like Marvel or DC would send to the distributors 	so they
could sell more comics.
I 	would look forward to 	just reading this 	catalog. 	It was 	almost 	like
I 	couldn't have cared less about the comics. 	The first thing I 	would
do is read this catalog. 	Even though there was no color in it, 	it was
black and white on newsprint-type paper, 	I 	would tear this thing open
and it's the first 	thing 	I'd read.
Basically it was 	just a catalog. 	It had all these different comics
that were going to be on sale for the next month. 	I'd put my order in
and know what I 	was getting, 	but there was something interesting about
this catalog. 	It was 	fun to read.
I 	mean yeah, 	I'm reading about here's what's 	in the next issue, 	but
the editor of this catalog used to kind of insert his own commentary.
He would be brutally honest about what he thought. 	It's almost like in
some cases he probably potentially lost sales.
He would say, 	"Okay, 	this 	is what's going to happen next month 	in this
next issue of Batman. 	Here we go again. 	Same old story as 	last month.
Are they going to do something different?" 	He would take little shots
at them and everything. 	He was 	just a witty, 	fun person to read. 	He
just made 	it fun 	to read this catalog. 	It was very entertaining.
It was 	almost 	like I'd 	just rather have read the catalog than half the
comics 	I 	got, 	and that's part one of the King of Selling Online, 	by
the way, 	is entertainment. 	It's actually a 	two-part thing, 	but I 	want
you to get a 	feel 	for 	this. 	Part one is 	just entertainment.
The entertainment aspect will set you apart from everyone else that
you're selling against, 	almost every single time. 	There's something
about entertainment that 	just attracts money and customers and even
fans.
Like I 	said, 	I 	was 	sitting there ordering comic books, 	getting them
two weeks 	late and probably a 	little bit more expensive because I 	was
probably paying shipping and handling at the time, 	and yet I 	didn't
care. 	I 	just wanted to get this catalog so I 	could read what this guy
was going to say about his comic books. 	He 	just always had something
interesting and funny to say, 	and it was 	just very entertaining. 	I
would have been happy 	just to get the catalog and nothing else. 	That's
how much I 	enjoyed it.
From what I 	understand, 	they were a very big mail-order comic book
company, 	so I 	couldn't have been the only one. 	There's 	just something
about that entertainment aspect. 	It 	just sells and it attracts people
to you, 	and it works even if you kind of 	suck at selling.

Again, 	this guy would say things 	and you'd look at it and say, 	nis he

because he knew you were going to buy it anyway, 	because if you were a
fan of Batman or Spider Man or whatever, 	you're going to buy it no
matter what he said, 	but he 	just kind of made it fun to read his
op1n1on on it, 	and he kind of made it fun to buy it. 	I 	mean he wasn't
even doing a 	selling 	job.
I'll give you some more examples of people who 	just kind of entertain
without giving value or teaching, 	without giving content or anything
else, 	and yet they sell. 	I 	used to live near Chicago in the suburbs
and I 	used to work in the city for 	a 	little bit when I 	got out of
college.
I 	was 	always 	amazed that you'd see people singing or doing a dance or
just something entertaining on the street for money, 	and people were
throwing these people money. 	They're not giving you any value or
anything, 	they're 	just kind of doing their thing. 	I 	guess 	in some ways
you'd almost want to call it begging, 	but they were doing it in an
entertaining way.
I 	remember once in the Bahamas on a trip, 	I 	was about 19 years old,
and I 	remember getting off the plane and there was 	some guy with a
guitar singing some songs 	from the Bahamas, 	and people were giving him
money. 	He really wasn't even doing anything but entertaining. 	He
wasn't trying to sell you anything. 	He had nothing to sell, 	he was
just trying to be entertaining, 	and people gave him money.
I 	don't watch much ESPN anymore, 	I 	just don't have time, 	but 	I 	used to
listen to it on the radio and there was this one guy who would not
even talk about sports 	for 	like an hour show. 	He wouldn't even barely
talk about sports. 	He would have all these guests on, 	pro athletes 	and
experts, 	but he would 	just rap with them about anything. 	Most of the
time he did not talk about 	sports. 	It was 	about their personal lives
or some event that happened to them or something, 	completely unrelated
to sports 	in any way.
Yet this 	show, 	from what I 	remember, 	had like the highest ratings of
its time slot. 	Nothing else even came close. 	You know what, 	it may not
even have been ESPN, 	I'm pretty sure it was them, 	but the point is
that whatever the network was actually fired the guy. 	The top brass
kept telling him, 	nLook, 	you've got to talk about sports."
He was 	like, 	nForget 	it. 	I 	know what my audience wants, 	and they want
to be entertained first. 	They're not watching me 	for 	sports
commentary. 	They want to be entertained about things 	relevant to their
favorite 	sports 	stars and that sort of thing."
This show 	just racked in the ratings, 	which meant money. 	He wasn't
selling anything. 	He wasn't really delivering any value. 	He wasn't
really delivering any content. 	He was 	just delivering entertainment.

I 	don't know exactly how it worked, 	but I 	believe after he got fired

Another example would be movies. 	I 	mean you go to the movies to be

there 	fighting this 	fight, 	and nobody even knows who he is because

Again, 	people crave entertainment 	first, 	but yes, 	they still want to
be taught and they still want content, 	so the second half 	is 	that
teaching and content.
Now you take the teaching and content, 	you add that to the
entertainment factor, 	and you get something called infotainment, 	which
you may have heard before, 	but that's what this whole thing is going
to be about is 	how to do this.
Infotainment, 	in my opinion, 	is the King of Selling Online. 	People who
use infotainment and know how to do it - 	and I'm going to show you 21
different ways of doing it 	just in this CD, 	so stick around - 	believe
me, 	after today you're going to have so much fodder to work with,

your competition in the ratings battle, 	so to speak. 	While they're
trying to out-content you, 	you're going to know how to use this 	little
thing called infotainment, 	which a 	lot of people have heard of, 	but
almost nobody knows how to do it.
Maybe you're wondering, 	uwhat does 	that really mean? What 	is
infotainment? Explain this 	to me." 	It's entertainment + content +
teaching. 	That's 	infotainment, 	but there's an even better definition
of this that I 	think will drive it home a 	lot better, 	which ironically
came from an entertainer I 	heard recently, 	the actor Alan Alda. 	You
may or may not have heard of him. 	He was 	in that TV show, 	M.A.S.H.,
about the Army surgeons in Korea. 	It was a very popular comedy 	for
many years.
He was 	being interviewed about the founder of 	60 Minutes, 	a guy named
Don Hewitt, 	who recently passed away. 	I 	guess they were good friends.
Alda basically described Don Hewitt's show as 	infotainment, 	but he
said it like this. 	He didn't say infotainment. 	He said basically,
uWhat they would do is be like giving someone a 	great-tasting hot dog
but that nourishes them like broccoli."
In other words, 	it tastes 	good, 	but it actually feeds you and sustains
like a very healthy food. 	It's wrapped up in a 	food 	that maybe doesn't
seem good for you, 	but that's 	fun to eat, 	but has the health benefits
of something that's good for you that maybe you wouldn't eat normally.
I 	just think that's a 	great way of explaining it. 	It's like giving
someone a 	great-tasting hot dog that nourishes them like broccoli.
This 	is easily applied to any kind of 	selling on the internet. 	I
really want to make this 	clear. 	I 	don't care if you're dealing with
article marketing or teleseminars, 	podcasts, 	videos, 	blogs, 	or emails.
I 	personally send an email out every weekday mostly to my 	list.
Believe me, 	it may look like I'm doing a 	lot of teaching, 	but I'm
really doing infotainment. 	There's very little that you're going to
get from my emails usually without actually buying the product that
I'm talking about.

I'm not saying I 	never teach and I 	never give hard content, 	but it's
very rare. 	I 	used to do it all the time, 	but when I 	started using
infotainment, 	my sales 	just shot up like crazy. 	I 	learned this 	secret.
And by the way, 	I 	need to give credit where credit is due on this. 	The
whole entertainment thing escaped me 	for many years. 	I 	kind of had it
figured out sort of, 	but there's 	so many people out there telling you
not to do this 	sort of thing.
They'll 	say, 	"Don't try to be funny, 	don't do that. 	Keep it serious.
It's business. 	There's nothing funny about making money," 	and blah
blah blah. 	You know, 	they're right in a way, 	but these are based on
rules that were kind of set down by old marketing masters so, 	60, 	70
years ago. 	Back then there wasn't this 	glut of content and information
and all that, 	so when you got some teaching or information it really
was a 	big deal, 	but now again we crave entertainment.
The man who really kind of brought this to my attention was a 	guy
named Matt Furey. 	This 	is 	something that he's 	just an expert at, 	and I
highly urge you to get on his 	list at www.MattFurey.com. 	I 	have to
give credit where credit is due on the entertainment part.
What we're going to do here is kind of build up on that seed that I
got from him, 	and I'm going to show you how I've turned it into making
a 	ton of 	sales, 	not 	just for my own site and things 	like that, 	but my
clients and businesses 	I 	partner with and that sort of thing. 	It 	just
makes a 	huge difference. 	It makes you stand out.
By the way, 	I 	was 	telling you about how there's certain rules that old
marketers put down. 	They were saying not to do this kind of thing in
sales 	letters either, 	but I 	can tell you right now, 	some of the best
sales 	letters 	I've written have an infotainment aspect to them. 	They
weren't necessarily rolling on the floor 	laugh your butt off type
stuff, 	but it was entertaining. 	They had entertainment value.
So do the sales 	letters that were created by the 	late copywriter, 	Gary
Halbert. 	If you read any of his 	really good sales 	letters, 	and he had
many of them, 	a 	lot of them were using infotainment. 	Now whether he
was doing it consciously or not, 	I 	don't know, 	but it's there and he's
one of the people I 	studied with.
You can also use infotainment when you're creating your products. 	In
fact, 	I 	almost insist on it. 	Again, 	I'm going to give you a perfect
example of this. 	One of my clients 	for 	the 	self-defense industry
created this product called Scientific Self-Defense. 	Unlike all these
other self-defense products, 	he hired real actors 	and he put himself
in it, 	and it basically was a 	series of 	like bar fight 	scenes.
He would be kind of with the cameras on him, 	and he'd simulate a
fight, 	like he was being attacked with a 	knife or a gun or a 	crowbar,
all these different scenarios. 	You felt 	like you were watching a
movie. 	It was 	scripted, 	the scenes were blocked, 	and it was all there.

They had professional cameramen and professional actors, 	who even went
so far 	as to study with real trauma units 	so they could effectively
react to being hit and everything like it's real 	life.
You feel 	like you're watching an action movie, 	a 	Bourne Identity type
movie, 	and that's how he describes 	it. 	The scenes were kind of funny
and they'd make these 	jokes and that sort of thing. 	They even did kind
of a spoof of the Star Wars cantina scene, 	which was 	kind of 	funny.
Then he'd stop the camera and go back and show you how he did it step-
by step. 	nsere's how I 	threw this punch, 	here's what I 	did with this
foot," 	and that sort of thing. 	The thing 	is 	it looked natural.
Of all of this guy's products, 	that is the one product that I 	watched
just one time and I 	learned more from that 	just about self-defense
than from all the other ones 	that were more dry teaching, 	like 	"Here's
how you do this move, 	here's how you do that move."
Because he packaged it in an entertaining format, 	infotainment, 	he not
only made his product much easier to consume and even fun to consume,
but he also did his customers a 	big service because it's much easier
to learn. 	In fact, 	I 	even wrote that 	in the ad -that the teaching is
kind of like in the movie The Matrix, 	where they plugged this 	thing
into the back of the main character's head and downloaded all of this
kung-fu moves and stuff. 	It 	just went right 	into his 	head and he knew
it.
It was 	a 	lot 	like that. 	You 	just kind of watched it unfold like a
movie, 	and it was very easy to see what he was doing. 	So I 	would say
use this 	in your product as much as you use it in your sales and
marketing. 	Plus 	it also sets up people wanting and looking forward to
your next product. 	It practically sells your next product for you, 	if
you do this right.
Think about it. 	If you do something in an infotainment format, 	I 	don't
care if it's an ebook or a video course, 	it really doesn't matter what
the product is, 	if you make it 	fun, 	you make 	it entertaining but also
very informational, 	people will 	look forward to it almost 	like they
look forward to a movie. 	They want more. 	Just give them more of what
they want and you'll be amazed at how this works. 	People will
literally anxiously await 	it.
Now we've gone over what infotainment is. 	I 	hope by now you're wanting
to give it a 	shot, 	because I 	really do believe that 	if you did nothing
but apply infotainment to everything you sell, 	you will get sales,
assuming there's 	people 	looking at your sales pitch and that sort of
thing. 	I 	really do believe 	it will make a 	huge difference 	in your
bottom line.
